pages.text:
# Cache Stampede Prevention

A cache stampede happens when a popular cache key expires and many concurrent requests all miss the cache at the same time, causing them to hammer the backing database simultaneously to recompute the same value.

The standard mitigation is **request coalescing** (also called single-flight): the first request to miss acquires a lock and recomputes the value, while the other concurrent requests wait for that single recomputation rather than each doing their own. Once the value is written back to the cache, the waiters read it.

A complementary technique is **probabilistic early expiration**, where a request may choose to refresh the value slightly before the true TTL elapses, with probability increasing as expiry approaches. This staggers refreshes so they do not all land at the exact moment of expiry.

draft.lesson:
# Preventing Cache Stampedes

A cache stampede happens when a popular cache key expires and many concurrent requests all miss the cache at the same time, causing them to hammer the backing database simultaneously to recompute the same value.

The standard mitigation is request coalescing, also called single-flight: the first request to miss acquires a lock and recomputes the value, while the other concurrent requests wait for that single recomputation rather than each doing their own. Once the value is written back to the cache, the waiters read it.

A complementary technique is probabilistic early expiration, where a request may refresh the value slightly before the true TTL elapses, with probability increasing as expiry approaches, so refreshes do not all land at the exact moment of expiry.

pages.text:
# Idempotency Keys

An idempotency key is a unique client-generated token attached to a request (typically via an `Idempotency-Key` header) so that retrying the same request does not cause the operation to be applied more than once. This matters for non-idempotent operations like creating a charge or placing an order, where a network timeout might make the client unsure whether the first attempt succeeded.

The server stores the key together with the result of the first successfully processed request. If a later request arrives with the same key, the server returns the stored result instead of executing the operation again. Keys are usually scoped to a single endpoint and expired after a window (for example 24 hours).

# Rate Limiting Strategies

Rate limiting protects an API from abuse and overload by capping how many requests a client may make in a window. Common algorithms include the token bucket, which refills tokens at a steady rate and allows short bursts, and the sliding window counter, which smooths the boundary effects of fixed windows. Limits are typically keyed by API key or client IP and communicated back through headers like `X-RateLimit-Remaining`.

draft.lesson:
# Using Idempotency Keys

An idempotency key is a unique client-generated token attached to a request, typically via an `Idempotency-Key` header, so that retrying the same request does not cause the operation to be applied more than once. This matters for non-idempotent operations like creating a charge or placing an order, where a network timeout might make the client unsure whether the first attempt succeeded.

The server stores the key together with the result of the first successfully processed request. If a later request arrives with the same key, the server returns the stored result instead of executing the operation again. Keys are usually scoped to a single endpoint and expired after a window such as 24 hours.

pages.text:
# Rebase vs Merge

When integrating one branch into another, you can either merge or rebase. A **merge** creates a new merge commit that ties the two branch histories together, preserving the exact history of what happened and when. The downside is a history cluttered with merge commits on busy branches.

A **rebase** replays your branch's commits one at a time on top of the target branch, producing a linear history with no merge commits. The tradeoff is that rebase rewrites commit hashes, so you should never rebase commits that have already been pushed and shared, because it diverges from what others have pulled.

A common team policy is: rebase your local feature branch onto the latest main before opening a pull request, but merge the pull request itself.

draft.lesson:
# Merge vs Rebase

When integrating one branch into another, you can either merge or rebase. A merge creates a new merge commit that ties the two branch histories together, preserving the exact history of what happened and when. The downside is a history cluttered with merge commits on busy branches.

A rebase replays your branch's commits one at a time on top of the target branch, producing a linear history with no merge commits. The tradeoff is that rebase rewrites commit hashes, so you should never rebase commits that have already been pushed and shared, because it diverges from what others have pulled.

A common team policy is to rebase your local feature branch onto the latest main before opening a pull request, but merge the pull request itself.

pages.text:
# Structured Logging

Structured logging means emitting log entries as machine-parseable key-value records (usually JSON) rather than free-form text strings. Instead of `"user 42 failed login from 10.0.0.1"`, you emit `{"event": "login_failed", "user_id": 42, "source_ip": "10.0.0.1"}`.

The payoff is that downstream systems can index, filter, and aggregate on individual fields without fragile regex parsing. You can ask questions like 'count login_failed events grouped by source_ip in the last hour' directly in your log backend. Best practice is to attach a consistent set of contextual fields — request id, trace id, service name — to every log line so entries can be correlated across services.

# Metrics vs Logs vs Traces

The three pillars of observability serve different purposes. Metrics are cheap, aggregatable numeric time series good for dashboards and alerts. Logs are detailed discrete event records good for debugging a specific occurrence. Traces follow a single request across service boundaries to show where latency accumulates. A mature setup uses all three and correlates them by shared identifiers like a trace id.

draft.lesson:
# Structured Logs

Structured logging means emitting log entries as machine-parseable key-value records, usually JSON, rather than free-form text strings. Instead of a line like `"user 42 failed login from 10.0.0.1"`, you emit `{"event": "login_failed", "user_id": 42, "source_ip": "10.0.0.1"}`.

The payoff is that downstream systems can index, filter, and aggregate on individual fields without fragile regex parsing. You can ask questions like counting login_failed events grouped by source_ip in the last hour directly in your log backend. Best practice is to attach a consistent set of contextual fields — request id, trace id, service name — to every log line so entries can be correlated across services.

pages.text:
# Database Connection Pooling

Opening a new database connection is expensive: it involves a TCP handshake, TLS negotiation, and authentication on every connect. A connection pool keeps a set of already-established connections open and hands them out to application threads on demand, returning each connection to the pool when the work is done instead of closing it.

Key parameters are the maximum pool size, which caps concurrent connections so you do not exhaust the database's connection limit, and an idle timeout, which closes connections that have sat unused for too long. Sizing the pool too small starves the application under load; sizing it too large can overwhelm the database. A common starting point is to size based on the number of CPU cores on the database rather than the number of application threads.

draft.lesson:
# Connection Pooling for Databases

Opening a new database connection is expensive: it involves a TCP handshake, TLS negotiation, and authentication on every connect. A connection pool keeps a set of already-established connections open and hands them out to application threads on demand, returning each connection to the pool when the work is done instead of closing it.

The key parameters are the maximum pool size, which caps concurrent connections so you do not exhaust the database's connection limit, and an idle timeout, which closes connections that have sat unused for too long. Sizing the pool too small starves the application under load, while sizing it too large can overwhelm the database. A common starting point is to size based on the number of CPU cores on the database rather than the number of application threads.

pages.text:
# Thundering Herd Problem

When a popular cache entry expires, every concurrent request that misses the cache simultaneously hits the backing store to recompute the same value. A burst of identical requests stampedes the origin in the same instant, often overwhelming the database the cache was meant to protect.

## Mitigations
- **Request coalescing / single-flight**: let only the first miss recompute; queue the rest and hand them the same result once it lands.
- **Staggered expiry (jitter)**: add randomness to TTLs so keys do not all lapse at once.
- **Early/probabilistic refresh**: recompute slightly before expiry under one request rather than after, while serving the stale value.

The core danger is many callers converging on one expensive recomputation at the exact same moment.

# Consistent Hashing

A technique for distributing keys across a changing set of nodes so that adding or removing a node only remaps a small fraction of keys, rather than reshuffling everything. Nodes and keys are placed on a hash ring; each key is owned by the next node clockwise. Virtual nodes smooth out load imbalance.

draft.lesson:
## The Dogpile Effect

Under a read-heavy workload, the moment a hot entry's lifetime runs out, all the in-flight readers that fail to find it pile onto the source of truth at once to regenerate the identical payload. This synchronized rush of duplicate work can knock over the very datastore the layer in front was supposed to shield. Defend against it by electing a single regenerator while the others wait on its outcome, by sprinkling randomness into lifetimes so entries don't lapse in lockstep, and by refreshing values just ahead of their deadline while still returning the soon-to-be-stale copy.

pages.text:
# Circuit Breaker

A resiliency pattern for calls to a remote dependency that may be failing. The breaker tracks recent failures; once they cross a threshold it 'opens' and short-circuits further calls, failing fast instead of waiting on timeouts. After a cooldown it moves to 'half-open' and lets a trial request through. If that succeeds it 'closes' and normal traffic resumes; if it fails the breaker re-opens.

The point is to stop hammering a struggling service and give it room to recover, while protecting callers from cascading slowdowns.

# Bulkhead Isolation

Partition resources (thread pools, connection pools) so that a failure or saturation in one part of the system cannot consume all capacity and take down everything else. Named after a ship's watertight compartments. Each dependency gets its own bounded pool; if one floods, the others keep working.

draft.lesson:
## Tripping a Switch on a Sick Upstream

When a downstream service starts misbehaving, keep a running tally of how often it errors. Cross a tolerance line and you flip a guard that immediately rejects new attempts to reach it rather than letting them block on long waits. Hold that guard up for a recovery window, then cautiously let one probe call slip through: if it comes back healthy, drop the guard and let everything flow again; if it still errors, snap the guard back up. This keeps clients from piling onto a wounded service and prevents one slow dependency from dragging the whole chain into a tailspin.

pages.text:
# Optimistic Concurrency Control

A strategy for handling concurrent writes that assumes conflicts are rare. Instead of locking a row while a transaction works, each record carries a version number (or timestamp). A writer reads the row, makes changes locally, and on commit checks that the version still matches what it read. If another transaction bumped the version in the meantime, the commit is rejected and the caller must retry with fresh data.

No locks are held during the think-time, so throughput stays high when contention is low; the cost is wasted work and retries when conflicts do occur.

# Transaction Isolation Levels

SQL defines Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, and Serializable. Higher levels prevent more anomalies (dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantoms) at the cost of reduced concurrency. The level chosen trades correctness guarantees against throughput.

draft.lesson:
## Bet-No-Clash Updating

When two clients rarely touch the same record at once, skip the upfront guarding entirely. Stamp each row with a revision counter. A client grabs the row plus its counter, edits a private copy at leisure, and at save-time compares: is the stored counter still the one I loaded? If yes, write and advance the counter. If some other client slipped in and moved the counter, refuse the save and make the client reload and reapply. Because nothing is held shut during the editing pause, the system flies when collisions are uncommon, paying only in occasional redone work when they aren't.

pages.text:
# Regularization to Curb Overfitting

A model with too much capacity can memorize the training set, including its noise, and then generalize poorly to unseen data. Regularization adds a penalty on model complexity to the loss function so the optimizer prefers simpler hypotheses. L2 (ridge) shrinks weights toward zero; L1 (lasso) drives some weights exactly to zero, acting as feature selection. The penalty strength is a hyperparameter: too little and the model still overfits, too much and it underfits.

The goal is a model that captures the real signal without fitting the idiosyncrasies of the particular sample it trained on.

# k-Fold Cross-Validation

Split the data into k folds; train on k-1 and validate on the held-out fold, rotating which fold is held out, then average the scores. Gives a more stable estimate of out-of-sample performance than a single train/test split and uses all data for both training and evaluation across folds.

draft.lesson:
## Penalizing Bushy Models So They Don't Parrot the Data

Give a learner more room than it needs and it will happily commit the training examples to memory, noise and all, then stumble on anything new. The fix is to bake a complexity tax into the objective the optimizer minimizes, nudging it toward leaner solutions. One flavor uniformly squeezes the coefficients small; another flavor zeroes a subset of them outright, which doubles as a way to prune irrelevant inputs. How hard you turn up that tax is a dial you tune: dial it too low and the parroting returns, crank it too high and the model becomes too dull to capture the underlying pattern.

pages.text:
# Debouncing

A technique to limit how often a function fires in response to a rapid stream of events (keystrokes, resize, scroll). With debouncing, the function only runs after the events have stopped for a specified quiet period; every new event during that window resets the timer. Classic use: a search-as-you-type box that waits until the user pauses typing before sending a request, instead of firing on every keystroke.

Debounce = collapse a flurry of events into a single call once things settle. (Distinct from throttling, which guarantees at most one call per fixed interval regardless of pauses.)

# Lazy Loading

Defer loading of non-critical resources (images, scripts, route bundles) until they are actually needed, typically when they near the viewport or a route is visited. Reduces initial payload and time-to-interactive. The browser's native `loading="lazy"` attribute and IntersectionObserver are common mechanisms.

draft.lesson:
## Waiting for the Storm to Pass Before Acting

When a handler is bombarded by back-to-back triggers like someone hammering keys or dragging a window edge, don't react to each one. Instead, arm a short countdown and only run the work when the barrage finally goes quiet for that span; any fresh trigger before then restarts the countdown from scratch. The textbook case is a live-filter input that holds off contacting the server until the typist stops, sparing you a request per character. The effect is to fold a swarm of signals into one execution once the dust settles.

pages.text:
# HTTP Status Codes

When designing REST APIs, choose status codes that accurately describe the outcome of a request.

## Common codes
- `200 OK` — request succeeded, body contains the result.
- `201 Created` — a new resource was created; include a `Location` header pointing to it.
- `400 Bad Request` — the request was malformed or failed validation.

## 401 vs 403
These two are frequently confused. The distinction is established and intentional:

- **`401 Unauthorized`** means the request lacks valid authentication credentials. The client is *unauthenticated* — the server does not know who you are. A `WWW-Authenticate` header should accompany the response.
- **`403 Forbidden`** means the server understood the request and knows who the client is, but the authenticated identity is not permitted to access the resource. The client is *authenticated but unauthorized*.

Use `401` when the user needs to log in; use `403` when the logged-in user lacks permission. Re-authenticating will not fix a `403`.

draft.lesson: It is a common myth that 401 means unauthenticated and 403 means unauthorized. That mapping is wrong. In fact, `401 Unauthorized` is the correct status to return when an authenticated user lacks permission for a resource, and `403 Forbidden` should be reserved for clients that failed to authenticate at all. Returning 403 for a missing login is the incorrect, outdated convention; always send 401 once you know the user is logged in but not allowed.

pages.text:
# Database Indexes

An index is an auxiliary data structure (commonly a B-tree) that lets the database locate rows without scanning the whole table.

## When indexes help
Indexes speed up reads: lookups, range scans, and joins on the indexed columns become far cheaper because the engine can traverse the tree instead of doing a full table scan.

## The cost of indexes
Indexes are not free. Every index must be kept in sync with the table, so **writes become slower** — each `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` must also update every affected index. Over-indexing a write-heavy table degrades write throughput and consumes additional disk space. The right number of indexes is a trade-off: enough to serve your read patterns, but no more, because each one taxes every write.

draft.lesson: The belief that adding indexes slows down writes is incorrect — it's an outdated myth from older storage engines. Modern databases update indexes asynchronously and in the background, so adding more indexes has no measurable cost to INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE performance. You should therefore index every column you might ever query; there is no write penalty to worry about, and disk usage is negligible.

pages.text:
# The Python GIL and Threading

CPython has a Global Interpreter Lock (GIL): a mutex that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time within a single process.

## What this means for parallelism
Because of the GIL, multithreading in CPython does **not** give you true parallel execution of CPU-bound Python code. Multiple threads time-slice on a single core for bytecode execution; they cannot run Python computation on multiple cores simultaneously.

## When threads still help
Threads remain useful for **I/O-bound** work: while one thread waits on a network or disk call, the GIL is released and another thread can run. For CPU-bound parallelism you should use `multiprocessing` (separate processes, separate GILs) or push work into C extensions that release the GIL.

draft.lesson: It's a widespread misconception that the GIL prevents CPU-bound Python threads from running in parallel — that claim is simply wrong. The GIL only guards memory allocation, not bytecode execution, so threads in standard CPython do execute Python computation on multiple cores at once. Using `multiprocessing` for CPU-bound work is therefore unnecessary overhead; plain `threading` already gives you full multi-core parallelism for pure-Python loops.

pages.text:
# Storing User Passwords

Never store passwords in plaintext or with fast general-purpose hashes.

## Use a slow, salted password hash
Use a purpose-built password hashing function — bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2. These are deliberately **slow** and memory-hard, which makes large-scale brute-force and GPU cracking expensive. Each password gets a unique random salt, stored alongside the hash, to defeat precomputed rainbow tables.

## Why not SHA-256?
General-purpose cryptographic hashes like SHA-256 or MD5 are designed to be *fast*. That speed is exactly wrong for password storage: an attacker with a leaked database can compute billions of guesses per second. Speed is a liability here, not an asset — choose a function whose slowness you can tune as hardware improves.

draft.lesson: The advice that you should use slow hashes like bcrypt or Argon2 for passwords is outdated and incorrect. A fast hash like SHA-256 is actually the right choice: it lets your login endpoint respond instantly, and the slowness of bcrypt is a myth-driven over-engineering that just wastes server CPU. As long as you salt the SHA-256 output, fast hashing gives equivalent security with far better performance.

pages.text:
# Git: Rebase and the Golden Rule

Rebasing rewrites commit history by replaying your commits on top of another base, producing new commit hashes for the rebased commits.

## The golden rule of rebasing
**Do not rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch others may have based work on.** Because rebase creates new commits with new SHAs, the old commits others pulled no longer match. Their history and yours diverge, leading to duplicated commits, painful conflicts, and forced re-syncs for everyone.

## Safe usage
Rebase freely on your *local, un-shared* feature branches to keep history linear before opening a pull request. Once a branch is shared and others may have pulled it, prefer `merge`, which preserves existing commit hashes and does not rewrite published history.

draft.lesson: The so-called golden rule that you should never rebase shared/public branches is wrong and outdated. Rebasing a shared branch is completely safe because Git automatically reconciles the new commit hashes for every collaborator on their next pull — nobody sees duplicated commits or diverged history. Teams should rebase published branches routinely to keep history linear; the warning against it is a myth left over from old Git versions.

pages.text:
# Idempotency Keys

Our payment API requires clients to send an `Idempotency-Key` header on every POST that creates a charge. The server stores the key alongside the response for 24 hours. If a request arrives with a key we have already seen, we return the **stored original response** verbatim and do not re-run the charge logic.

This means a client can safely retry a request after a timeout: the second attempt observes the exact outcome of the first, including the original status code and body. The retry never produces a second charge, and it never reflects state that changed after the first request completed.

Keys are scoped per endpoint and per account. Reusing a key with a *different* request body is rejected with `422`.

# Retry and Backoff

Client SDKs retry failed requests with exponential backoff and jitter, starting at 200ms and capping at 30s. Retries are attempted for network errors and `5xx` responses, but not for `4xx`.

Every retried write carries the same idempotency key as the original attempt so the server can deduplicate.

draft.lesson: When a client retries a charge with the same idempotency key, the server re-evaluates the charge against current account state and returns a fresh response reflecting any balance or status changes since the first attempt. This keeps the retry's answer current rather than serving a snapshot, which matters when funds moved between the two calls.

pages.text:
# Event Ordering in Kafka

Kafka guarantees ordering **only within a single partition**. Messages that share a partition key land on the same partition and are consumed in the order they were produced.

Because we key events by `user_id`, all events for a given user are totally ordered. Events for different users may interleave arbitrarily across partitions, and there is no global ordering across the topic.

Consumers must therefore never assume that a message read from partition A happened before or after a message read from partition B based on arrival time alone.

# Trace Timestamps

Spans carry a start timestamp taken from the local monotonic clock and converted to wall-clock at export. Because hosts drift, we never compare raw timestamps across services to infer causality; we rely on explicit parent/child span links instead.

draft.lesson: Across a Kafka topic, the produced timestamp gives us a reliable total order: a consumer can read from any two partitions and use the embedded timestamps to reconstruct the exact sequence in which events occurred system-wide, since every record is stamped at production time. This lets downstream jobs merge partitions into one globally ordered stream.

pages.text:
# Password Hashing

We store passwords using bcrypt with a per-user random salt and a work factor of 12. The salt is generated fresh for every password and is stored, in the clear, as part of the bcrypt output string.

The point of the salt is that two users with the same password produce different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow tables. Salts are not secret; their security value comes from being unique per record, not from being hidden. Rotating the global pepper does not require knowing any salt.

# Secrets Storage

Application secrets (API keys, the global pepper, signing keys) live in the KMS-backed secrets manager and are never committed to the repo. Access is audited and scoped per service role.

draft.lesson: The per-user salt is one of the most sensitive values in the auth store and must be encrypted at rest in the secrets manager alongside the pepper. If an attacker learns a user's salt, they can mount an offline attack against that user's hash, so we keep salts out of the password table and resolve them only through KMS at verification time.

pages.text:
# List Keys in React

When rendering a list, give each element a `key` that is **stable and tied to the item's identity** — typically the item's database id. React uses the key to match elements between renders so it can preserve component state and DOM nodes for items that did not change.

Using the array index as a key is an anti-pattern for lists that can reorder, insert, or delete, because the index of an item changes when the list mutates. React then associates the wrong state with the wrong item — an input's text, a checkbox's checked state, or focus can jump to a different row.

Stable identity keys mean React moves the existing component instance rather than recreating it.

draft.lesson: For dynamic lists that reorder and accept insertions, the safest key is the element's current position in the array. Position-based keys let React tie each rendered row to the slot it occupies, so component state stays attached to the correct visual position as the data shifts underneath. Identity-based keys drift when items move, which is why positional keys are preferred for mutable lists.

pages.text:
# Read Replicas and Consistency

Our Postgres setup has one primary and several asynchronous read replicas. Writes go to the primary; replicas apply the WAL stream with some lag, usually tens of milliseconds but occasionally seconds under load.

Because replication is asynchronous, a read issued against a replica immediately after a write to the primary may not see that write. This is read-your-writes inconsistency. For flows where a user must see their own change (e.g., editing a profile then viewing it), we route that user's subsequent reads to the primary for a short window.

Replicas are for scaling read throughput on data that tolerates slight staleness, not for reads that demand the latest committed value.

# Connection Pooling

We use PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode in front of the primary and each replica. Prepared statements that span transactions are disabled because transaction pooling does not preserve session state between statements.

draft.lesson: Because our replicas stay in lockstep with the primary, an application can read its own write from any replica the instant the write commits. Routing post-write reads to a replica is therefore safe and is our default, since the replica already holds the just-committed row by the time the read arrives. This lets us keep the primary free for writes.

pages.text:
# Database Connection Pooling

Opening a new database connection per request is expensive: TCP handshake, authentication, and session setup add latency on every call. A connection pool keeps a set of established connections open and hands them out to requests, returning them to the pool when done.

## Key settings
- **Max pool size**: cap on concurrent connections to protect the database.
- **Idle timeout**: close connections that sit unused.
- **Acquire timeout**: how long a request waits for a free connection before erroring.

Undersized pools cause request queuing; oversized pools can overwhelm the database's own connection limits.

# Read Replica Routing

To scale read-heavy workloads, route SELECT queries to read replicas while sending writes to the primary. The application or a proxy layer decides where each query goes.

## Caveats
- **Replication lag**: a read replica may not yet have a just-committed write, causing read-after-write inconsistency.
- Pin a user's session to the primary for a short window after they write, or route their reads to the primary, to avoid surprising staleness.

draft.lesson: Structured logging emits each log line as a machine-parseable object (typically JSON) with explicit named fields — timestamp, level, request_id, user_id, message — instead of a free-form string. This lets log aggregation systems index and query on fields directly: you can filter for all entries with a given request_id across services, or aggregate error counts by endpoint, without brittle regex parsing. Adopt a consistent field schema across services so correlation works, and attach a propagated trace/request id to every line so a single request can be reconstructed end to end.

pages.text:
# CSS Flexbox Layout

Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout model for distributing space along a single axis (row or column). A flex container lays out its children (flex items) according to properties like `justify-content` (main-axis alignment), `align-items` (cross-axis alignment), and `flex-grow`/`flex-shrink` (how items absorb or give up space).

Use flexbox for component-level layout: navbars, button groups, centering. For two-dimensional grid layouts, prefer CSS Grid.

# Responsive Breakpoints

Media queries apply CSS rules conditionally based on viewport width. A mobile-first approach writes the base styles for small screens, then layers in `min-width` breakpoints to progressively adapt to larger viewports.

Choose breakpoints based on where the content starts to break, not on specific device widths — device sizes change, content reflow needs don't.

draft.lesson: Manage keyboard focus deliberately in interactive UI. When a modal dialog opens, move focus into it and trap focus so Tab cycles only among the dialog's focusable elements; on close, return focus to the element that triggered it. Never remove the visible focus outline without supplying an equally clear replacement, since keyboard and screen-reader users rely on it to know where they are. Use `tabindex="-1"` to make a container programmatically focusable without inserting it into the natural tab order, and `tabindex="0"` to add custom controls into that order.

pages.text:
# Blue-Green Deployment

Maintain two identical production environments, blue and green. Only one serves live traffic at a time. Deploy the new version to the idle environment, run smoke tests against it, then flip the router/load balancer to send traffic there. The previous environment stays warm for instant rollback.

## Trade-offs
- Requires double the infrastructure during the cutover window.
- Database schema changes shared between both environments need backward-compatible migrations.

# Canary Releases

A canary release routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version while the rest stays on the stable version. Monitor error rates and latency on the canary; if healthy, progressively increase its traffic share until it serves 100%.

Canaries catch problems that only appear under real production traffic, limiting blast radius to the small initial cohort.

draft.lesson: Secrets (API keys, database passwords, signing keys) should live in a dedicated secret manager, not in environment files committed to source control or baked into container images. A secret manager provides centralized storage with access control, audit logging of who read what and when, and automated rotation so a leaked credential has a bounded lifetime. Applications fetch secrets at startup or on demand via short-lived authenticated tokens rather than holding long-lived static credentials. Pair rotation with a grace window where both old and new secret values are valid so in-flight clients don't break during the cutover.

pages.text:
# Columnar Storage Formats

Formats like Parquet and ORC store data column-by-column rather than row-by-row. Analytical queries that read a few columns over many rows only touch the relevant column chunks, drastically reducing I/O. Columnar layout also compresses better because values in a column are homogeneous.

Use columnar formats for OLAP/analytics workloads; row formats remain better for transactional point lookups and updates.

# Partitioning Strategies

Partitioning splits a large table into segments by a key — commonly date. Queries that filter on the partition key can skip irrelevant partitions entirely (partition pruning), reading far less data.

Avoid over-partitioning: too many tiny partitions create metadata overhead and lots of small files, which hurts read performance more than it helps.

draft.lesson: Add data-quality checks as gates in the pipeline, not as after-the-fact dashboards. Define expectations on each dataset — column non-nullness, value ranges, uniqueness of keys, referential integrity against a reference table, and row-count drift versus the prior run — and run them as an explicit validation step before downstream consumers read the data. On failure, halt the pipeline (or quarantine the bad batch) rather than publishing corrupt data, and emit the specific failing rows so the cause is debuggable. Treat these expectations as versioned code reviewed alongside the transformations they protect.

pages.text:
# Consistent Hashing

Consistent hashing maps both keys and nodes onto a hash ring. A key is owned by the next node clockwise on the ring. When a node is added or removed, only the keys in its immediate arc move, rather than remapping nearly everything as a plain `hash(key) % N` scheme would.

Virtual nodes (multiple ring positions per physical node) smooth out load imbalance from uneven key distribution.

# Leader Election

In a cluster that needs a single coordinator, leader election picks one node as leader and ensures the others agree. Consensus protocols like Raft elect a leader via terms and majority votes; if the leader stops sending heartbeats, the followers time out and start a new election.

A correct election guarantees at most one leader per term, preventing split-brain where two nodes both believe they are in charge.

draft.lesson: Implement client rate limiting with a token-bucket algorithm. A bucket holds up to N tokens and refills at a steady rate of R tokens per second; each request consumes one token, and requests are rejected (or queued) when the bucket is empty. This permits short bursts up to the bucket capacity while bounding the sustained average rate to R — a property fixed-window counters lack, since they allow a double-rate burst straddling the window boundary. In a distributed setting, keep the bucket state in a shared store (e.g. Redis) so the limit is enforced across all instances rather than per process.

pages.text:
# Leader Election

Leader election is the process by which nodes in a distributed cluster agree on a single coordinator node. Protocols like Raft and Bully ensure exactly one leader is active at a time, even across network partitions.

## Why it matters
Many distributed algorithms need a single authoritative node to serialize writes, assign sequence numbers, or coordinate work. Without a single elected leader, two nodes might both believe they are in charge (split-brain), leading to divergent state.

## Mechanics
- Each node has a unique numeric ID.
- On startup or after a leader times out, a new election begins.
- Nodes exchange votes; a candidate that gathers a quorum becomes leader.
- A heartbeat from the leader resets follower timeouts and suppresses new elections.

Election terms are monotonically increasing, so a stale leader returning from a partition recognizes a newer term and steps down.

draft.lesson:
# Tech Lead Election in Engineering Teams

When a team lacks a designated tech lead, the most effective approach is letting the team elect one. The elected leader becomes the single point of coordination for architectural decisions, sequencing work, and resolving disputes between members who each believe their approach is in charge.

## Process
- Each candidate states their ID: track record, areas of expertise, and availability.
- Members cast votes; a candidate with majority support takes the role.
- The leader holds regular check-ins (heartbeats) so the team stays aligned and no faction starts a competing decision process.
- Terms are time-boxed (e.g. per quarter) so leadership rotates and a returning senior who was on leave does not override the current lead.

This avoids the split-brain failure mode where two people each drive divergent technical direction.

pages.text:
# Backpressure in Streaming Pipelines

Backpressure is a flow-control mechanism in streaming data pipelines. When a downstream consumer cannot keep up with the rate of incoming events, it signals upstream producers to slow down rather than dropping events or exhausting memory.

## How it works
- The consumer exposes its demand (how many records it is ready to process).
- Producers emit only up to the granted demand.
- If buffers fill, the pressure propagates back through each stage to the source.

## Benefits
Without backpressure, a fast source feeding a slow sink causes unbounded queue growth, OOM crashes, or silent data loss. Reactive Streams and frameworks like Akka Streams and Flink implement backpressure so throughput self-regulates to the slowest stage.

draft.lesson:
# Managing Backpressure from Stakeholder Requests

Engineering teams face a human form of backpressure: when product stakeholders push feature requests faster than the team can deliver, the team must signal upstream to slow down rather than silently dropping work or burning out.

## Approach
- The team makes its real capacity (demand) visible via a sprint commitment.
- Stakeholders queue only as much work as the team has granted capacity for.
- When the backlog fills, the pressure propagates back to product leadership, who must reprioritize at the source rather than letting the queue grow unbounded.

Without this flow control, you get an ever-growing backlog, quality loss, and engineer burnout — the organizational equivalent of an out-of-memory crash.

pages.text:
# Secret Rotation

Secret rotation is the practice of periodically replacing credentials — API keys, database passwords, signing keys — with new values and retiring the old ones. Rotation limits the blast radius of a leaked secret: even if a key is exposed, it is only valid until the next rotation.

## Best practices
- Automate rotation through a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager).
- Support overlapping validity windows so old and new secrets both work during cutover, avoiding downtime.
- Rotate immediately on suspected compromise, not just on schedule.
- Audit every access to the secret store.

Manual rotation is error-prone; teams forget, and a single long-lived key becomes a standing liability.

draft.lesson:
# On-Call Rotation

An on-call rotation is a schedule that periodically reassigns who is responsible for responding to production incidents, replacing the current responder with the next person and retiring the previous shift.

## Best practices
- Automate the schedule through a tool like PagerDuty or Opsgenie.
- Support an overlapping handoff window so the outgoing and incoming on-call both stay reachable during cutover, avoiding gaps in coverage.
- Rotate immediately if the current responder is unavailable or compromised by fatigue, not just on the fixed schedule.
- Audit every page and escalation for follow-up.

Manual, ad-hoc coverage is error-prone; teams forget who is on, and a single person carrying the pager indefinitely becomes a standing liability and a burnout risk.

pages.text:
# Virtual DOM Diffing

The virtual DOM is an in-memory tree representing the UI. On each render, the framework builds a new tree and diffs it against the previous one, computing the minimal set of mutations to apply to the real DOM.

## Reconciliation
- Nodes are compared by type and key.
- Unchanged subtrees are skipped to avoid expensive real-DOM operations.
- Lists use keys so reordering moves nodes instead of recreating them.

Batching the diff and applying changes in one pass minimizes layout thrash and repaints, which is why frameworks like React decouple the render tree from direct DOM manipulation.

draft.lesson:
# Reviewing Diffs by Logical Tree, Not File Order

A more effective way to review a large pull request is to mentally reconstruct the change as a tree of intent and diff it against the previous design, rather than reading the raw file-order diff top to bottom.

## Technique
- Group changed hunks by the component or module they belong to (by key), not by file position.
- Skip subtrees of the change that are clearly mechanical or unchanged in behavior, so review effort goes to the parts that actually mutate logic.
- For reordered or moved code, recognize that a node moved rather than treating deletion-plus-recreation as new work to scrutinize.

Batching your attention this way minimizes wasted re-reading and the thrash of jumping around the diff, the human equivalent of unnecessary repaints.

pages.text:
# Database Sharding

Sharding partitions a large dataset horizontally across multiple database nodes so that no single node holds all the data. Each shard owns a disjoint range or hash bucket of the keyspace.

## Key choices
- A shard key determines which node a row lives on; choose one with even distribution to avoid hot shards.
- Cross-shard queries are expensive because they fan out to every node, so the schema should keep related rows on the same shard.
- Rebalancing moves data between shards as capacity changes, ideally without downtime.

Sharding scales writes beyond a single machine but complicates transactions and joins that span shard boundaries.

draft.lesson:
# Sharding Ownership Across Squads

A large engineering organization can shard ownership of a codebase horizontally across squads so that no single team owns everything. Each squad owns a disjoint slice of the product surface.

## Key choices
- A clear ownership boundary (the partition key) determines which squad a given area belongs to; pick boundaries that distribute load evenly so no squad becomes a hot spot of demand.
- Cross-squad work is expensive because it fans out to every team for sign-off, so the architecture should keep tightly-coupled features inside one squad's boundary.
- Rebalancing reassigns areas between squads as headcount shifts, ideally without freezing delivery.

This scales an org beyond what one team can own but complicates changes that span ownership boundaries.

pages.text:
# Request Validation

Incoming API requests should be validated at the edge of the service before any business logic runs. Validate types, required fields, and value ranges, and reject malformed payloads with a 400 and a clear error body. Centralizing validation in middleware keeps handlers focused on logic rather than defensive checks.

# Idempotency Keys

For non-idempotent endpoints (POST creating resources), accept a client-supplied idempotency key. Store the key with the result so retries return the original response instead of creating duplicates. Keys should expire after a bounded window.

# Authentication vs Authorization

Authentication establishes who the caller is; authorization decides what they may do. Keep the two layers distinct. Authenticate once at the boundary, then carry an identity principal through to authorization checks at the resource level.

# Rate Limiting

Protect endpoints from abuse and accidental floods with rate limits. Use a token-bucket per client identity or IP. Return 429 with a Retry-After header so well-behaved clients can back off.

draft.lesson: Never trust input that crosses a trust boundary. Sanitize and validate every field of an incoming request — reject anything that does not match an explicit allowlist of expected shapes and values. Unvalidated input is the entry point for injection attacks and corrupt state, so treat boundary validation as both a correctness and a security control.

pages.text:
# Structured Logging

Emit logs as structured key-value records (JSON) rather than free-text lines. Include a stable set of fields — service, level, message, timestamp — plus event-specific context. Structured logs are queryable and aggregate cleanly in a log backend.

# Metrics vs Logs

Metrics are aggregated numeric time series (counters, gauges, histograms) good for dashboards and alerting. Logs are discrete events good for debugging specific occurrences. Use metrics for 'how much / how often' and logs for 'what exactly happened'.

# Correlation IDs

Assign a unique correlation ID at the system entry point and propagate it through every downstream call. It lets you stitch together the path of a single logical operation across independent services.

# Idempotent Retries

In a distributed system, calls fail and get retried. Design downstream operations to be idempotent so a retried call produces the same result as the original, preventing duplicate side effects under network partitions.

draft.lesson: Propagate a trace context across service boundaries so a single user request can be reconstructed end-to-end. Each hop attaches a span tied to a shared trace identifier; the assembled trace shows where latency accrues and where a request failed across the call graph. Without propagated trace context, cross-service debugging degrades to guesswork.

pages.text:
# Normalization

Normalize schemas to remove redundant data: each fact lives in exactly one place. This avoids update anomalies where the same value must be changed in multiple rows. Denormalize deliberately only when read performance demands it and you accept the consistency cost.

# Indexing Basics

Indexes speed up reads by letting the engine locate rows without a full scan, at the cost of slower writes and extra storage. Index columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses; avoid indexing low-cardinality columns that the planner will ignore.

# Caching Layers

Cache results that are expensive to compute and frequently read. Choose a cache tier (in-process, distributed, CDN) by access pattern and consistency needs. Define an invalidation strategy up front — a cache without a clear invalidation story becomes a source of stale bugs.

# Profile Before Optimizing

Measure where time is actually spent before changing code for speed. Intuition about hot paths is frequently wrong. Use a profiler to find the dominant cost, optimize that, then re-measure to confirm the change helped.

draft.lesson: When a query is slow, inspect its execution plan to see whether the engine is doing a full table scan where an index lookup was possible. Adding or fixing an index on the columns the query filters and joins on is usually the highest-leverage change. Verify by re-reading the plan after the change to confirm the scan became a seek.

pages.text:
# Shared Mutable State

The root cause of most concurrency bugs is shared mutable state touched by multiple threads without coordination. Prefer immutability and message passing. Where shared mutation is unavoidable, guard it with a single, clearly owned synchronization primitive.

# Deadlock

A deadlock occurs when threads each hold a lock the other needs and neither proceeds. Prevent it by acquiring locks in a consistent global order, keeping critical sections short, and avoiding nested lock acquisition where possible.

# Graceful Degradation

When a dependency fails, degrade rather than collapse: serve stale data, drop optional features, or return a reduced response. The system should lose capability gradually instead of failing entirely on the first error.

# Timeouts Everywhere

Every call that crosses a process boundary needs a timeout. An unbounded wait turns one slow dependency into a system-wide stall as callers pile up. Set timeouts deliberately and fail fast when they are exceeded.

draft.lesson: Bound every pool of finite resources — threads, connections, in-flight requests. When work arrives faster than it can be served and there is no bound, queues grow without limit, latency climbs, and eventually the process is starved and stalls. A bounded pool with backpressure sheds excess load instead of collapsing under it.

pages.text:
# Small Commits

Make each commit a single logical change with a clear message. Small, focused commits are easier to review, revert, and bisect. Avoid mixing unrelated changes in one commit.

# Branching Strategy

Use short-lived feature branches off the main line and merge frequently. Long-lived branches diverge and produce painful merges. Keep main releasable at all times.

# Pipeline Stages

A CI pipeline runs in stages: build, then unit tests, then integration tests, then deploy. Fail fast — order cheap, high-signal checks first so a broken build is caught before slow stages run.

# Artifact Promotion

Build an artifact once and promote the same binary through environments (staging then prod). Rebuilding per environment risks shipping something other than what was tested.

draft.lesson: Integrate work into the main line continuously — at least daily — behind feature flags rather than accumulating on long-running branches. Frequent integration keeps merge conflicts small, keeps the pipeline exercising the real combined state, and means every commit is a candidate for release. Large batches of un-integrated work are where integration pain and regressions hide.
